questions.q: IEEE 754 single-precision floats have 1 sign bit, 8 exponent bits, and 23 mantissa bits. How many significant decimal digits does this represent?
questions.explanation: With 23 mantissa bits, the precision is 2^{-23} ≈ 1.2 × 10^{-7}. This gives about 6-7 significant decimal digits. Double-precision (52 mantissa bits) gives ≈15-16 digits.

questions.q: Machine epsilon (ε_machine) is the smallest relative error in rounding a real number. For IEEE 754 double-precision, ε_machine ≈:
questions.explanation: For double-precision with 52 mantissa bits, ε_machine = 2^{-52} ≈ 2.22 × 10^{-16}. Single-precision gives 2^{-23} ≈ 1.19 × 10^{-7}. Machine epsilon bounds the relative rounding error in basic operations.

questions.q: Catastrophic cancellation occurs when subtracting two nearly equal numbers. Why is this numerically problematic?
options: Leading significant digits cancel, leaving mostly rounding error; relative error becomes huge
The CPU runs out of memory during the computation
The exponent becomes negative and underflows to zero
The result is always exactly zero
questions.explanation: If a ≈ b, then a - b has few significant digits but is computed from two numbers with many. Rounding error in a and b (relative error ε_machine × |a|) dominates the tiny result, yielding relative error ≈ ε_machine × |a| / (a - b), which is huge.

questions.q: To avoid catastrophic cancellation in computing √(1 + x) - 1 for small x > 0, the recommended form is:
questions.explanation: Direct computation √(1 + x) - 1 cancels when √(1 + x) ≈ 1. Multiplying by the conjugate gives [1 + x - 1] / [√(1 + x) + 1] = x / [√(1 + x) + 1]. The denominator is not near-unity, so cancellation is avoided.

questions.q: In IEEE 754, numbers are normalized so the mantissa satisfies 1 ≤ m < 2. What is the representation of the number x = 5.5?
questions.explanation: 5.5 = (101.1)₂ = 1.011 × 2² in binary. The mantissa is 1.375 = (1.011)₂ and exponent is 2. The leading 1 in the mantissa is implicit in IEEE 754, so only .011 is stored.

questions.q: Subnormal (denormalized) numbers in IEEE 754 have exponent field all zeros. What is their smallest magnitude?
questions.explanation: Subnormal numbers use an implicit exponent (typically 2^{-126} for single-precision) but mantissa < 1, allowing gradual underflow. The smallest positive subnormal is 2^{-126} × 2^{-23} = 2^{-149} ≈ 1.4 × 10^{-45}.

questions.q: What happens in IEEE 754 when overflow occurs (result is too large)?
options: The result is set to infinity (±∞) and an exception is signaled
The result is rounded to the largest finite float
An error is raised and the program terminates
The result wraps around to the smallest positive float
questions.explanation: IEEE 754 specifies overflow returns ±∞ depending on sign. Infinity can propagate through further computations. Underflow returns zero or subnormal numbers. Both are recoverable errors that allow graceful degradation.

questions.q: For computing a sum S = x₁ + x₂ + ... + x_n with very different magnitudes, the Kahan summation algorithm improves accuracy by:
options: Keeping a running correction term to track rounding error and add it back in subsequent iterations
Sorting terms in increasing order of magnitude before summing
Skipping very small terms to avoid underflow
questions.explanation: Kahan summation maintains c (accumulated rounding error) and computes y = x_i - c, then S' = S + y, then c = (S' - S) - y. This correction reduces error from O(nε_machine |S|) to O(ε_machine |S|), independent of n.

questions.q: The relative error in representing a real number x by a float fl(x) satisfies |fl(x) - x| / |x| ≤ ε_machine (assuming no underflow). This is a consequence of:
options: Rounding to the nearest representable float; the error is at most half a unit in the last place (ULP)
The mantissa having a fixed number of bits
The binary base used in IEEE 754
questions.explanation: IEEE 754 rounds to nearest: |fl(x) - x| ≤ 0.5 × ulp(x) = 0.5 × 2^{-p} × 2^e where p is precision bits and 2^e is the scale. Relative error |fl(x) - x| / |x| ≤ (0.5 × 2^{-p} × 2^e) / (2^e × mantissa) ≈ ε_machine.

questions.q: When implementing the discriminant formula x = [-b ± √(b² - 4ac)] / (2a) for a quadratic, numerical instability occurs when b² >> 4ac and b > 0. Why?
options: The formula -b + √(b² - 4ac) subtracts two nearly equal large numbers, causing catastrophic cancellation
questions.explanation: When b > 0 and 4ac << b², we have √(b² - 4ac) ≈ b, so -b + √(b² - 4ac) cancels. The stable approach is compute one root as r₁ = [-b - √(b² - 4ac)] / (2a) (no cancellation), then r₂ = c / (a·r₁).
